note: Each landmark places Hazium's first flag (the earliest annual cutoff where it entered the top-20 riskiest of ~5,900 substances, from the stabilized HEWB v1.4 run under strict known_at discipline) against independent, dated milestones that Hazium does not consume as features. The literature-volume and ECHA CLH-intention features are model inputs, so they are deliberately absent here: comparing against them would be circular. Regulatory and public-awareness dates are hand-verified against the enacting act, EFSA output, or a documented public controversy, cited per marker. A public marker appears only where a substance had a genuine dated public controversy (the neonicotinoid bee campaign, the US chlorpyrifos ban); most landmarks had no public profile at all when Hazium flagged them, and carry no such marker. GDELT news-media volume is ingested as an independent axis, but its index only reaches back to 2017, after every landmark's pre-2017 story, so these historical public markers are hand-curated and GDELT's live value is the present-day watchlist. Each row is labeled with the tightest threshold it actually reached (top-10, top-20, or top-50); k=20 is the primary bar because the strict top-10 line is sensitive to fold-level noise for borderline substances, while top-20 is stable.

outcomes.clean_lead: Hazium ranked it top-tier years before the first independent concern.
outcomes.ahead_of_eu_action: Hazium flagged it before the first EU-wide restriction, though national concern was already emerging.
outcomes.level: Hazium and the regulator's assessment moved at roughly the same time.
outcomes.behind: The regulator restricted it before Hazium's model flagged it.
outcomes.miss: Hazium never flagged it before its real action.

landmarks.name: Chlorpyrifos

markers.label: US EPA proposes to revoke all tolerances (public controversy)
EFSA statement: human-health concerns, DNT and genotoxicity

landmarks.note: Ranked second-riskiest of the whole population at the first cutoff tested. It was already flagged years before the substance became a US public controversy in 2015, and roughly a decade before EFSA's 2019 concern. The most robust of the ten: consistently the highest-ranked landmark.

landmarks.name: Thiacloprid

markers.label: EFSA peer review: reprotoxic 1B, endocrine-disruptor criteria met

landmarks.note: Top-8 riskiest in 2010, about nine years before EFSA's peer review concluded the reprotoxic and endocrine concerns. No public profile: a substance the public never debated.

landmarks.name: Chlorpyrifos-methyl
landmarks.note: Sister compound to chlorpyrifos; top-3 riskiest in 2011, about eight years before the EFSA concern statement. It rode along with chlorpyrifos in the regulatory decision but had little public profile of its own.

landmarks.name: Mancozeb
landmarks.note: Top-20 from 2010, roughly nine years before EFSA's endocrine finding and about eleven years before its 2021 non-renewal (Reg 2020/2087).

landmarks.name: Propikonazol
landmarks.note: Reached the top-50 at the first cutoff tested, roughly eight years before EFSA's conclusion. It never entered the top-20, so this is a top-50 flag, shown as such. No public profile.

landmarks.name: Clothianidin

markers.label: Public bee campaign (2.5M-signature petition, mass coverage)

landmarks.note: Top-10 riskiest in 2010, before the public bee campaign of 2012 and about three years before the EU-wide 2013 restriction. National bans and EFSA bee-risk work were already underway around 2008 to 2012, so Hazium was early against EU-wide action, with national concern already building.

landmarks.name: Thiamethoxam

markers.label: Public bee campaign (2.5M-signature petition, mass coverage)

landmarks.note: Top-20 from the first cutoff tested, before the 2012 public campaign and about four years before the 2013 restriction. It only reaches the strict top-10 in 2019, so this is a top-20 flag: robustly early at that bar, borderline at the tighter one.

landmarks.name: Imidacloprid

markers.label: Public bee campaign (2.5M-signature petition, mass coverage)

landmarks.note: The counterexample among the neonicotinoids: Hazium only reached the top-20 in 2017, years after the 2012 public campaign and the 2013 EU restriction. It flagged this one late, following the regulatory wave.

landmarks.name: Dimethoate

markers.label: EFSA peer review: genotoxicity of omethoate metabolite

landmarks.note: Entered the top-10 in early 2019, a few months after EFSA's 2018 peer-review conclusion and about five months before the ban. Hazium moved in step with the regulator here. No public profile.

landmarks.name: Epoxiconazole
landmarks.note: The one landmark the model does not flag before its real action, at any k.

landmarks.name: Fluazinam

markers.label: Swedish national reevaluation (outside the EU non-renewal process)
markers.source: The anchor case; a Swedish national concern

landmarks.note: The original case that motivated Hazium. Its 2025 concern is national groundwater and TFA contamination; the EU-regulatory, hazard, and sales sources ingested so far do not cover groundwater or residue monitoring, which is the next data source on the roadmap.
